summary: Ancient Greek philosopher, student of Socrates, retroactively called the founder of Cynicism by Diogenes Laertius. Argued that virtue, not pleasure, was the end of existence. His Physicus asserted monotheism. Diogenes of Sinope was called his 'faithful hound.'

works.name: Founding of Cynicism
works.description: Antisthenes is retroactively called the founder of Cynicism by Diogenes Laertius. He was a student of Socrates who argued that virtue, not pleasure, was the end of existence. His anti-hedonist stance is captured by the quote 'I would rather go mad than feel pleasure.' He advocated askesis (discipline) alongside the later Stoics. Diogenes of Sinope was called his 'faithful hound' because he doggedly pursued Antisthenes.

clues.clue: Student of Socrates; retroactively called founder of Cynicism by Diogenes Laertius

clues.clue: Physicus asserted only one God, unknowable by humans

works.name: Plato's 'Featherless Biped' Incident
works.description: Antisthenes is contextually linked to the famous incident where Diogenes plucked a chicken to refute Plato's definition of man as a 'featherless biped,' since Antisthenes taught Diogenes. Dog-like behavior of Cynics inspired the school's name. Antisthenes's followers from Maroneia included Metrocles and Hipparchia.

clues.clue: Plato's 'featherless biped' definition refuted by Diogenes (student of Antisthenes) with a plucked chicken
examples: Diogenes brought one of these things to the Academy and proclaimed 'Behold! A man!'

clues.clue: Dog-like following of Antisthenes may have inspired the name Cynicism
examples: his dog-like following of this earlier thinker may have inspired the name of Cynicism

comprehensive_summary:
Antisthenes was an ancient Greek philosopher and student of Socrates who is retroactively credited as the founder of Cynicism by the biographer Diogenes Laertius. He argued strongly that virtue, not pleasure, was the ultimate end of existence, famously declaring 'I would rather go mad than feel pleasure.' His work Physicus asserted a form of monotheism, claiming there is only one God who is unknowable by humans.

Diogenes of Sinope was called Antisthenes's 'faithful hound' because he doggedly pursued him, and this dog-like following may have inspired the very name of the Cynic school (from the Greek for 'dog-like'). Antisthenes's philosophical lineage connects Socrates to Diogenes and ultimately to the broader Cynic movement, which included Crates of Thebes, Hipparchia of Maroneia, and her brother Metrocles. The school he founded advocated askesis (discipline of mind and body), a practice later adopted by the Stoics.

Antisthenes appears primarily in bonus parts rather than tossups, typically as the answer to questions about the founding of Cynicism or as context for Diogenes's antics. His main clue identifiers are: student of Socrates, founder of Cynicism, anti-hedonist, and teacher of Diogenes.
